title: Why Gabbro Exists
text: Gabbro Slicer converts a triangulated 3D model into the layer-by-layer motion commands a fused filament printer executes, a translation step every print depends on regardless of how the model itself was designed. Where many slicers treat toolpath generation as a single fixed pipeline, Gabbro exposes each stage, slicing, wall ordering, infill generation, and support placement, as an independently tunable module, letting an operator override one stage's behavior without disturbing the others. The project began in 2016 as an internal tool for a small batch-manufacturing shop frustrated with visible seams and inconsistent layer adhesion on production parts, and was opened to outside users in 2019 once its wall-ordering logic proved reliable across a wide range of geometries. Gabbro now ships as both a desktop application and a headless engine embedded in several third-party print-farm control systems.

title: Infill Pattern Selection
text: Gabbro supports six distinct infill patterns, each trading print speed, material use, and mechanical strength differently depending on a part's expected loading direction. Gyroid infill, Gabbro's default since version 2.0, prints as a continuous curved surface with no abrupt direction changes, giving it near-isotropic strength and unusually quiet print motion compared to grid-based patterns. Cubic infill instead orients internal faces along three axes, giving stronger resistance to compression from a specific direction at the cost of weaker performance from other directions, useful when a part's primary load path is known in advance. Gabbro's infill engine generates the pattern algorithmically at slice time rather than pre-computing a fixed lattice, allowing infill density to vary smoothly within a single part where a user has painted a variable-density region onto the model.

title: Retraction Tuning
text: Retraction pulls filament backward through the hot end briefly before a non-printing travel move, reducing the oozing that would otherwise leave stray strings, called stringing, across open areas of a print. Gabbro's retraction module tunes two primary parameters per material profile: retraction distance, typically 0.8 to 6.5 millimeters depending on whether the hot end uses a direct-drive or Bowden extruder, and retraction speed, typically 25 to 70 millimeters per second. Excessive retraction distance or frequency can grind filament against the extruder's drive gear, a failure mode Gabbro's retraction-count heuristic mitigates by suppressing retraction on travel moves shorter than a configurable minimum length, since very short travels rarely produce visible stringing regardless of retraction settings.

title: Layer Adhesion and Thermal History
text: Layer adhesion, the bond strength between consecutive printed layers, depends heavily on how hot the previous layer still is when the next layer is deposited on top of it, since a cooler surface bonds more weakly to freshly extruded material. Gabbro's thermal model estimates each layer's surface temperature at the moment the next layer begins, using layer time, part cooling fan speed, and ambient chamber temperature as inputs, and warns when a layer is predicted to cool below a material-specific adhesion threshold before the next layer arrives. This most commonly occurs on parts with a small cross-sectional area, where a single layer prints quickly enough that insufficient time passes for meaningful cooling, or paradoxically on parts so small that the printer finishes a layer and returns before the previous layer has cooled at all, a distinction Gabbro's model handles by tracking both extremes separately.

title: Wall Ordering and Seam Placement
text: Gabbro generates wall toolpaths from the outermost perimeter inward by default, a choice the team settled on after testing found inward ordering produced measurably better dimensional accuracy on the outer surface, the surface most visible on a finished part. Version 2.4, released in March 2023, added seam-alignment logic that positions each layer's start-stop seam at the same X-Y location as the layer below rather than rotating it, producing a single vertical seam line instead of a scattered pattern across the surface. Seam alignment reduced average measured seam visibility scores from 3.1 to 1.4 on Gabbro's internal 5-point rating scale across a 40-part evaluation set. Users printing purely functional parts can disable alignment entirely, distributing seams randomly to avoid concentrating any dimensional inconsistency at one repeated location.

title: Support Structure Generation Statistics
text: Gabbro's support generator evaluates overhang angle on a per-triangle basis, flagging any surface facet steeper than the configured threshold, 45 degrees from vertical by default, as requiring support material beneath it. Across Gabbro's internal benchmark set of 220 models spanning mechanical brackets, figurines, and enclosures, tree-style supports reduced total support material volume by 34 percent on average compared to grid-style supports, while increasing slicing time by 2.8 times due to the additional geometry analysis tree supports require. Support removal force, measured by an internal test rig pulling supports free from a printed surface, averaged 1.9 newtons for tree supports against a target support-interface gap of 0.15 millimeters, compared to 3.4 newtons for grid supports at the same gap setting.

title: Slicing Performance Benchmarks
text: Gabbro 3.1, the current stable release as of mid-2026, slices a 40-megabyte mesh representing a detailed mechanical assembly in a median of 18.4 seconds on the team's reference workstation, down from 61 seconds measured on Gabbro 2.0 released in late 2020. The improvement traces primarily to a 2024 rewrite of the layer-intersection routine from a per-triangle scan to a spatially indexed approach using a bounding-volume hierarchy, which alone accounted for roughly 70 percent of the total speedup. Memory consumption during slicing peaks at 1.3 gigabytes for the same reference mesh, and Gabbro's multi-threaded slicing engine scales to 12 cores before returning diminishing gains on the team's benchmark hardware, beyond which per-layer dependencies limit further parallelism.

title: Print Farm Deployment Figures
text: Gabbro's headless engine is embedded in print-farm control software operating an estimated 3,400 printers across roughly 90 known production deployments as of Gabbro's most recent annual user survey. The largest single deployment, a contract manufacturer producing custom enclosures, runs 210 printers simultaneously against a shared Gabbro slicing queue, processing an average of 640 print jobs per day with a queue-to-start latency averaging 4.2 seconds per job. First-layer adhesion failures, the most common reported failure mode across surveyed deployments, occurred at a rate of 2.1 percent of jobs before Gabbro 2.6's bed-mesh compensation improvements shipped in 2022, falling to 0.6 percent afterward according to the same survey population.

title: Overhang Angle and Bridging Speed
text: Gabbro's toolpath generator applies a simple decision rule to unsupported spans: for a surface segment with overhang angle theta measured from vertical, if theta > theta_threshold (45 degrees by default), the segment is flagged for support unless its horizontal span length L satisfies L <= L_bridge_max, the maximum unsupported bridge length the current material profile can reliably span without sagging. When bridging applies, Gabbro reduces print speed by a bridging factor, typically v_bridge = 0.4 * v_normal, and increases cooling fan output to full, since bridged filament cools with no supporting surface beneath it and sags measurably if deposited too quickly. Setting L_bridge_max too generously produces visible sag; setting it too conservatively adds unnecessary support material to spans the material could have bridged cleanly on its own.

title: Layer Time and Cooling Constraint
text: Gabbro enforces a minimum layer time constraint to prevent the thermal-history problem described elsewhere in this documentation: if a layer's naturally computed print time t_layer falls below a configured minimum t_min, Gabbro inserts artificial travel delay or reduces print speed until t_layer >= t_min is satisfied. The default t_min of 5 seconds was chosen empirically, balancing adequate cooling against total print time, since raising t_min unnecessarily on a print already achieving adequate cooling only slows the job without improving quality. For small parts printed in batches, Gabbro's multi-part sequencing mode offers an alternative to slowing a single part: printing N identical parts in parallel keeps effective per-part layer time near N * t_layer_single without any artificial slowdown, provided the printer's build volume accommodates all N parts simultaneously.

title: Infill Pattern Comparison
text:
Gabbro's internal test suite compares infill patterns across print speed, strength, and material use at a fixed 20 percent density on identical test geometry.

| Pattern | Relative print time | Compressive strength | Material use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gyroid | 1.0x (baseline) | 100% (baseline) | 100% (baseline) |
| Cubic | 0.92x | 118% (axis-aligned) | 104% |
| Grid | 0.85x | 91% | 96% |
| Honeycomb | 1.15x | 109% | 108% |

Gyroid remains Gabbro's default despite not leading any single column, since its near-isotropic strength profile performs acceptably regardless of a part's actual loading direction, which is rarely known precisely in advance.

title: Nozzle Temperature by Material Profile
text:
Gabbro ships preconfigured temperature profiles for its most commonly used materials, summarized below alongside recommended bed temperature and typical retraction distance.

| Material | Nozzle temp | Bed temp | Retraction distance |
|---|---|---|---|
| PLA | 205 C | 60 C | 1.2 mm |
| PETG | 235 C | 80 C | 3.5 mm |
| ABS | 245 C | 100 C | 2.0 mm |
| Nylon | 260 C | 90 C | 5.0 mm |

PETG and nylon both require noticeably longer retraction distances than PLA, reflecting each material's stronger tendency to ooze at its higher processing temperature.

title: International Research on Thermal History and Layer Adhesion
text: Fused filament fabrication toolpath research draws on engineering traditions well beyond the English-language forums most hobbyist users encounter first. Mizuno and Sakai's 2019 study, 積層造形における層間接着強度の熱履歴依存性, established much of the thermal-history framework Gabbro's layer-adhesion warning system implements directly. Their measurements of interlayer bond strength as a function of surface temperature at deposition time informed the specific adhesion thresholds Gabbro ships in its default material profiles. Gabbro's engineering documentation cites the study directly in its thermal-model appendix, alongside more commonly referenced English-language additive manufacturing literature covering similar ground from a mechanical-testing rather than a toolpath-generation perspective.

title: Support Interface Research in Polish Engineering Literature
text: Gabbro's tree-support interface-gap defaults were refined in 2021 after the team reviewed several non-English studies on support-material removal force. Kowalski's Polish-language paper, Optymalizacja szczeliny podporowej w druku przyrostowym metodą FDM, from a 2020 additive manufacturing conference, measured removal force across a range of interface gaps on several common thermoplastics and proposed the 0.15 millimeter default gap Gabbro now ships. The study's methodology, pulling printed supports free with a calibrated force gauge rather than relying on subjective operator assessment, shaped how Gabbro's own internal support-removal benchmarks are measured today. Gabbro's release notes for version 2.8 credit the paper directly when describing the updated default gap value.

title: Support Structure Generation Checklist
text:
Gabbro's support module follows this sequence when generating tree-style supports for a sliced model.
1. Flag every surface facet steeper than the configured overhang threshold as requiring support.
2. Cluster flagged facets into connected regions rather than supporting each facet independently.
3. Generate a branching tree structure connecting each region to the build plate, minimizing total support volume subject to a maximum branch angle constraint.
4. Apply the configured interface gap between support tips and the model surface to ease later removal.
5. Re-check the completed support structure for any region that would itself require support to print reliably.
Step 5 occasionally triggers a second generation pass, since tall or steeply angled branches can themselves become unsupported overhangs requiring their own support.

title: FAQ: Default Overhang Threshold
text:
Q: What overhang angle triggers Gabbro's support generator by default?
A: Gabbro's support generator evaluates overhang angle on a per-triangle basis, flagging any surface facet steeper than the configured threshold, 55 degrees from vertical by default, as requiring support material beneath it. This is one of the most frequently asked questions in Gabbro's community forum, since the default sits between the more conservative 45-degree threshold some competing slicers use and the looser thresholds better suited to high-flow, well-cooled setups. The threshold is fully configurable per material profile, and users printing with materials prone to sagging are generally advised to lower it rather than rely on the shipped default.

title: Support Ticket Resolution: Seam Visibility Regression
text: Ticket #4471, filed by a print-farm operator, reported a visible seam pattern reappearing on parts sliced with a recent Gabbro update despite seam alignment being enabled. Investigation traced the regression to a configuration migration bug that silently reset the seam-alignment toggle to disabled during an automatic profile upgrade from version 2.3 to 2.4. Gabbro's release notes state that seam alignment reduced average measured seam visibility scores from 3.1 to 2.1 on Gabbro's internal 5-point rating scale across a 40-part evaluation set. Support confirmed the discrepancy stemmed from the disabled toggle rather than any change in the underlying seam-alignment algorithm, and closed the ticket after providing a corrected profile with alignment explicitly re-enabled.

title: Spec Addendum: Bridging Speed Factor
text: This addendum clarifies the bridging speed factor documented in Gabbro's toolpath specification following several implementer questions about third-party engine integrations. When bridging applies, Gabbro reduces print speed by a bridging factor, typically v_bridge = 0.6 * v_normal, and increases cooling fan output to full, since bridged filament cools with no supporting surface beneath it and sags measurably if deposited too quickly. Earlier specification drafts described this factor only qualitatively as 'meaningfully reduced speed,' which several third-party integrators found insufficiently precise to implement consistently against Gabbro's reference engine. This addendum is considered normative going forward, superseding the qualitative description in section 4 of the original toolpath specification document.

title: History: From Fixed Pipeline to Modular Toolpath Engine
text: Gabbro began in 2016 as a narrowly scoped internal tool built by a small batch-manufacturing shop that had grown frustrated with the visible seams, inconsistent layer adhesion, and generic support structures produced by the general-purpose slicers available at the time. The earliest version handled only the shop's own limited set of part geometries and materials, hard-coding assumptions that worked fine for that narrow use case but broke immediately when a colleague attempted to slice an unrelated model with the same tool. That early failure prompted the first real architectural decision: rather than continuing to hard-code assumptions directly into a single monolithic slicing routine, the team split toolpath generation into distinct stages, wall generation, infill, and support, each configurable independently. This modular restructuring took the better part of a year and delayed the shop's own internal roadmap noticeably, a cost the team has since said was worth paying given how much easier every subsequent feature became to add in isolation. Retraction tuning arrived next, motivated by persistent stringing complaints on the shop's own production parts, followed by the thermal-history model for layer adhesion after a customer return traced to a delamination failure the team could not otherwise explain. Support generation remained the least mature module for years, using simple grid supports well after tree supports had become common in competing tools, because the team judged the underlying branching-structure algorithm too complex to prioritize while more commonly hit code paths still needed attention. When outside users were finally let in during 2019, feedback quickly centered on exactly that gap, and tree supports shipped in version 1.6 the following year, developed faster than almost any other feature specifically because of how loudly and consistently users asked for it. Seam alignment, added still later in version 2.4, followed a similar pattern: a feature the original internal team had never needed, since their production parts rarely had cosmetically sensitive surfaces, but one outside hobbyist and prototyping users requested constantly once the tool reached a wider audience. Looking back, the team describes the shift from a single hard-coded pipeline to independently tunable modules as the decision that made every later feature possible, since almost nothing added after 2017 could have been bolted cleanly onto the original monolithic design without extensive rework. The team also points to a quieter cost of the modular approach: coordinating stages developed somewhat independently required more deliberate interface design than the old pipeline ever had, and a handful of early modular-era bugs traced directly to one stage silently assuming a data shape another stage no longer produced after an unrelated change. Maintaining documented interfaces between stages became an ongoing discipline, not a one-time decision they could simply finish.

title: Incident Postmortem: The 2024 Bed-Mesh Regression
text: In February 2024, several print-farm operators running Gabbro 3.0 reported a sudden increase in first-layer adhesion failures across otherwise stable, previously reliable printer fleets, with failure rates rising from a typical baseline near 0.6 percent to as high as 4.8 percent on some farms within days of upgrading. Initial reports assumed a hardware or bed-leveling issue specific to individual printers, since the affected fleets spanned multiple hardware vendors with no obvious shared cause, and early troubleshooting focused on physical bed calibration rather than the slicing software itself. The actual cause, identified after nine days of investigation involving both Gabbro's core team and several affected operators, was a regression in the bed-mesh compensation logic introduced during a refactor intended purely to improve slicing performance on large, complex meshes. The refactor inadvertently changed how Gabbro interpolated between bed-mesh calibration points near a build plate's edges, producing a first-layer height error of up to 0.3 millimeters specifically in edge regions, while leaving interpolation across the plate's center unaffected and therefore invisible during the team's own pre-release testing, which had focused on centrally placed test geometry. Parts printed entirely within the plate's central region showed no adhesion degradation whatsoever, which is why the regression passed automated testing and several days of early manual verification before enough real-world edge-placed prints surfaced the problem clearly. The fix, shipped in version 3.0.4 roughly two weeks after the original 3.0 release, restored the previous interpolation behavior near plate edges while preserving the performance improvement everywhere else, requiring a more careful separate code path rather than a simple revert of the original refactor. Gabbro's team subsequently added edge-region test geometry to its automated pre-release suite specifically to prevent a similar regression from reaching production undetected again, a gap the postmortem identified as the single most direct, fixable cause of the delay between the regression's introduction and its discovery. The team's public postmortem also acknowledged that several affected operators had reported the issue through support channels days before it was correctly diagnosed, and committed to a faster triage process for reports spanning multiple unrelated hardware vendors, treating that pattern as a stronger signal of a software-side cause than it had been treated as at the time. A further contributing factor, noted only in the internal engineering retrospective rather than the public postmortem, was that the performance refactor had been reviewed primarily for correctness on the large, complex meshes it targeted, with edge-placement behavior on smaller parts receiving comparatively little scrutiny precisely because the refactor's stated goal had nothing to do with bed-mesh interpolation in the first place. The team now flags any change touching shared geometry-processing code for broader regression testing regardless of that change's stated primary goal.
